transcript.transcript: Interview with Molly Bennett. Side two. The kind of migrants who were coming over in the 50s and 60s. Do you think that that's changed the kind of people who were coming in the 50s and 60s? Yes, it's changed because younger people, this is a global situation when the older men would be very happy to run and come on the farm, because some of those men could be easily be farmers themselves. That would be glad to come and make some US dollars. The younger kids today don't so much gravitate to want to run and do that, because we want to work in sugar cane. We want to go and work in shade tobacco under that eat the younger kids today and I've seen it for myself. They want to wear the sneakers Sneak because like in America, the clothes like in America, and they don't see the working aspect of it. They figure, yes, coming to America. But when they think they have to work for menial money, then it is a detergent to what they really want to do, you know. You know, if they were coming and getting the money, then. But so true that now you don't see them so much gravitating to say yes, they want to come like the old time Jamaican that truly want to come and make some extra money to send their kids to school, maybe to buy farm material for their own self, you know? So what are they doing instead? Sit in the corner, open the cut a tune and make some big wad of money, or do whatever else is the going thing for the day in the fast money making, you know. And what does this mean for the kind of West Indian community that's going to emerge in the next 20 years as these people are coming of age. Are very worried about it because as I travel, I used to travel Eastern Caribbean with my husband. Um, and when I say my country, Jamaica, and I see these kids, they have two kids that go to school, one in the morning and one in the afternoon. And when you're at the bus stop and you see on dreads, any part of the island is especially in the corporate area, I see hundreds of kids. You wonder where they're going to find a job, what is going to happen? It's frightful. No factories and what make it so hard? When we were younger coming up, we had. You could go. You could migrate to London, England. You could go to Canada. You could come to America. Because there was one time when the housework open up domestic work and those people could get their families to come. But now everywhere is locked down. So I don't know what's going to happen to the masses. What organizations did you join. As a little girl? There wasn't enough. You mean in America here? Yes. Oh, I go to church. I have little time to join a lot of organization, more than put a lot of things in the church. And I help out with, um, the CNA program that my husband started, that I helped with that and active in the church. My lifestyle don't leave me much because when my husband was alive, I gave him all the time that he wanted to do a good job at what he wanted. And I work in the business and the building that we have like 24, seven, 24 hours a day, seven days a week. We have we built this building and the building has been rented now from 1973 until now, at this moment, it's filled and we don't have a manager. We, the Bennetts, Molly Bennett and the children and good help have maintained this building, a four story building. So my affiliation with a lot of organization. I am a member of the Council of Negro Women, but I can't put a lot of time in it. I do support them. Council of Negro Women and, um. Do you have any relationship with the West Indian Social Club. I support whatever they do. But as again, I tell you, I can't really wrapped up in, um, endear, you know, be there 24 hours. I'm not a social butterfly to, you know, and I'm up in age now. I can't take a lot of the activities. What about earlier on in the. When I was younger? Yeah, yeah. Were you a dues paying member and your husband as well? Well, not with the West Indian Club. My husband was he was the president for five years of the Caribbean Club. The Caribbean, American. Caribbean and Mortensen. And what's so distinctive about the Caribbean American society from the West Indian social. Well, the club the Caribbean had their own agenda and the West Indian Club at their own agenda and do different things such as? Well, they have their own program that they run know up there at the West Indian Club and the Caribbean. I don't want to gender in sponsoring. They have the beauty queen giving money for scholarships and all of that. You know, whatever they see fit and want to do. No, I'm asking because I'm trying to get a sense of what specific role each of the different West Indian or Caribbean organizations, um, play. I wanted to ask you about what you saw as some of the major problems dividing the West Indian community and how what they can do to make things better for themselves in terms of having like, one voice as West Indians. Well, nothing is impossible. We could all try. You and form a link and let it work. Form one link and let it work. But do you think that one organization can address the needs of the different sections of the West Indian population? I should think so, because as the saying go, divide and conquer, and we would not want to have no splinter group as West Indian. We would want to have one accord, one for all. But what are the factors working against that? Well, I really don't know. What are the factors. I really don't know what the factors are because it's not any one thing that you could say. This is number one thing, so we don't want it. It's everybody having their own opinion which you're entitled to, your own opinion. But is it good or bad? Is it in the best interest of whatever we are doing? You see what I'm saying? So we need to be of one accord. Well, specifically for you, what does it what does it mean to be a Jamaican woman? Or what does it mean to be West Indian in the Hartford community? Well, I personally, Molly Bennett, don't single out myself as being a Jamaican woman, a West Indian woman. I'm just one woman in this society working for the benefit of all people. I have to serve a cultural, diversified form of people every day in my life. If pink, black, green, you name it, everybody and I have to have a smile for everyone. But if, uh, if so many of you know now that the West Indian population has grown, if so many people are moving out of the Hartford area, moving into the suburbs, working in in the suburban areas, what is it that's going to bring them together as West Indians specifically, if they're in such a multicultural environment and they have to interact with so many different kinds of people. Well, since, you know, since we have all this diversity, I don't see there's any reason why we can't get we get together when, for instance, if there's something going on, for instance, West Indian Week, we get together for whatever reason, whether it's the music, whether it's the fellowship, whether they have our services, whatever it is, the party or whatever, we can get together for the other things. Just just as big, loud and clear. Hundreds of people from all over come at independence. I don't see where it would be impossible that we can't get a quarter of that same people to be united and do the right thing in the right area. What sort of things would you like to see accomplished in the area for West Indians or by West Indians specifically? Well, I would like to see a very strong present politically. I would like to see a strong present politically, so that you could sit down with the powers to be Year and make a difference for your own self, for the community, so that the other people will know we are here. What would like, what we can do and whatever it takes to be good people. Because if you don't cultivate good people, you'll always have that. Set of people that you don't want to be aligned with. What is it that, um, that makes African Americans and West Indians vote in bloc for Democrats and not for Republicans? I asked because your husband ran on a Republican ticket, which isn't very common for a lot of. Well, I, I really don't study the politics too much, but from what I know, you have to have a two party system, Which is very good, because if one person should have the monopoly, whether it be politically or it's like was the telephone company when they have the monopoly. Sooner or later that break down and give everybody a fair chance and another voice is heard. You just cannot have anything one sided and just lump it up and believe you must take it. I don't think it goes very well. We must have a challenge. But so many of the blacks seems to be lumped up in the Democratic Party. Well, I suppose this go on for century, you know, and some. You see, when my husband I remember vividly 30 odd years ago, you could not say you were a Republican because they would think you are crazy. But then again, you have to take the challenge and you have to do what he thought, and he did what he think he wanted to do and did it, and not because he was Republican. It was hurting anybody. He went out and fight for everyone. Get along with Democrats, Republicans, and try to make Hartford a better place to live. While he was here, and I'm sure if anybody is not biased and you meet at least ten people, two people will, says Colin Bennett did try. Colin Bennett did help. He was not selfish and sticking party line and said, well, you are Democrat and I'm Republican. He went and help everyone, and I don't think the city of Hartford is any worse off for what has been done. We tried, and after you have done your best and tried what more else there is, and I'm here and I'm not holding a party line. I just do each day what I know I think is right and help whoever I can. And so it is. Okay. Thank you.

lines.line: # Changing Attitudes Toward Migration and Farm Work

> The speaker describes how the demographics and motivations of migrants have shifted over time. While older generations of men were eager to work on farms in the United States to earn money and support their families, younger people today are less interested in such labor. The younger generation aspires to American styles and material goods but is deterred by the prospect of hard, low-paying work, leading to a decline in enthusiasm for traditional migrant labor opportunities.

metadata.title: Changing Attitudes Toward Migration and Farm Work
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes how the demographics and motivations of migrants have shifted over time. While older generations of men were eager to work on farms in the United States to earn money and support their families, younger people today are less interested in such labor. The younger generation aspires to American styles and material goods but is deterred by the prospect of hard, low-paying work, leading to a decline in enthusiasm for traditional migrant labor opportunities.

lines.line: # Youth Choices and Economic Realities in the Caribbean

> Instead of seeking farm work abroad, many young people in the Caribbean are turning to alternative, often informal, ways of making money. The speaker expresses concern about the lack of direction and the pursuit of quick financial gains among youth. This shift reflects broader economic and social changes, as traditional migration routes and employment opportunities become less accessible.

metadata.title: Youth Choices and Economic Realities in the Caribbean
metadata.synopsis: Instead of seeking farm work abroad, many young people in the Caribbean are turning to alternative, often informal, ways of making money. The speaker expresses concern about the lack of direction and the pursuit of quick financial gains among youth. This shift reflects broader economic and social changes, as traditional migration routes and employment opportunities become less accessible.

lines.line: # Limited Migration and Economic Challenges for Caribbean Youth

> The speaker shares her worries about the future of Caribbean youth, noting the scarcity of jobs and the closure of traditional migration pathways to places like England, Canada, and the United States. She observes the large numbers of young people with limited prospects and highlights the lack of factories and employment opportunities. The speaker contrasts the current situation with earlier times when migration for domestic or farm work was more feasible, expressing uncertainty about what will happen to the growing population of unemployed youth.

metadata.title: Limited Migration and Economic Challenges for Caribbean Youth

She observes the large numbers of young people with limited prospects and highlights the lack of factories and employment opportunities. The speaker contrasts the current situation with earlier times when migration for domestic or farm work was more feasible, expressing uncertainty about what will happen to the growing population of unemployed youth.

lines.line: # Personal Involvement in Church and Community Activities

> The speaker discusses her limited involvement in organizations outside of her church due to time constraints and family responsibilities. She describes her active participation in church programs and her support for her husband's initiatives, such as the CNA program. The speaker also mentions managing a family-owned building, which has required significant dedication and left little time for broader organizational involvement, though she supports groups like the Council of Negro Women.

metadata.title: Personal Involvement in Church and Community Activities
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses her limited involvement in organizations outside of her church due to time constraints and family responsibilities. She describes her active participation in church programs and her support for her husband's initiatives, such as the CNA program. The speaker also mentions managing a family-owned building, which has required significant dedication and left little time for broader organizational involvement, though she supports groups like the Council of Negro Women.

lines.line: # Engagement with West Indian and Caribbean Organizations

> The conversation turns to the speaker's and her husband's involvement with West Indian and Caribbean organizations. While she supports the West Indian Social Club, her participation is limited by her busy schedule and age. Her husband, however, was more deeply involved, serving as president of the Caribbean Club. The speaker notes that different organizations have their own agendas and programs, such as scholarships and cultural events, distinguishing them from one another.

metadata.title: Engagement with West Indian and Caribbean Organizations

While she supports the West Indian Social Club, her participation is limited by her busy schedule and age. Her husband, however, was more deeply involved, serving as president of the Caribbean Club. The speaker notes that different organizations have their own agendas and programs, such as scholarships and cultural events, distinguishing them from one another.

lines.line: # Unity and Division Among West Indian Organizations

> The interviewer probes the reasons behind divisions within the West Indian community and the potential for unity. The speaker expresses hope that a single, unified organization could serve the needs of all West Indians, emphasizing the importance of avoiding splinter groups. She acknowledges that differing opinions and interests can make unity challenging, but stresses the value of working together for the common good.

metadata.title: Unity and Division Among West Indian Organizations
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer probes the reasons behind divisions within the West Indian community and the potential for unity. The speaker expresses hope that a single, unified organization could serve the needs of all West Indians, emphasizing the importance of avoiding splinter groups. She acknowledges that differing opinions and interests can make unity challenging, but stresses the value of working together for the common good.

lines.line: # Identity and Community Service in a Diverse Society

> When asked about her identity as a Jamaican or West Indian woman in Hartford, the speaker downplays ethnic distinctions, seeing herself as a woman working for the benefit of all people. She describes her daily interactions with a culturally diverse population and emphasizes the importance of treating everyone with kindness and respect, regardless of background.

metadata.title: Identity and Community Service in a Diverse Society

She describes her daily interactions with a culturally diverse population and emphasizes the importance of treating everyone with kindness and respect, regardless of background.

lines.line: # Maintaining West Indian Community Ties Amid Diversity and Suburbanization

> The interviewer raises concerns about maintaining West Indian community cohesion as people move to the suburbs and interact with a broader range of cultures. The speaker believes that despite increased diversity, West Indians can still come together for cultural events, celebrations, and shared interests. She points to large gatherings during West Indian Week and independence celebrations as evidence that unity is possible, and suggests that similar efforts could foster greater community solidarity.

metadata.title: Maintaining West Indian Community Ties Amid Diversity and Suburbanization
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer raises concerns about maintaining West Indian community cohesion as people move to the suburbs and interact with a broader range of cultures. The speaker believes that despite increased diversity, West Indians can still come together for cultural events, celebrations, and shared interests. She points to large gatherings during West Indian Week and independence celebrations as evidence that unity is possible, and suggests that similar efforts could foster greater community solidarity.

lines.line: # Aspirations for Political Presence and Community Development

> The speaker expresses a desire for a stronger political presence for West Indians, so they can advocate for their needs and contribute positively to the wider community. She emphasizes the importance of cultivating good people and being recognized as valuable members of society. The speaker believes that political engagement and community development are key to improving the status and influence of West Indians in the area.

metadata.title: Aspirations for Political Presence and Community Development
metadata.synopsis: The speaker expresses a desire for a stronger political presence for West Indians, so they can advocate for their needs and contribute positively to the wider community. She emphasizes the importance of cultivating good people and being recognized as valuable members of society. The speaker believes that political engagement and community development are key to improving the status and influence of West Indians in the area.

lines.line: # Political Affiliation and Voting Patterns in the Black Community

> The interviewer asks about the tendency of African Americans and West Indians to vote for Democrats, noting the speaker's husband's unusual choice to run as a Republican. The speaker reflects on the importance of a two-party system and the need for political competition. She recalls a time when being a Republican was stigmatized in the community, but argues that having multiple perspectives and choices is essential for a healthy democracy.

metadata.title: Political Affiliation and Voting Patterns in the Black Community
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer asks about the tendency of African Americans and West Indians to vote for Democrats, noting the speaker's husband's unusual choice to run as a Republican. The speaker reflects on the importance of a two-party system and the need for political competition. She recalls a time when being a Republican was stigmatized in the community, but argues that having multiple perspectives and choices is essential for a healthy democracy.

lines.line: # Reflections on Political Involvement and Community Legacy

> The speaker reflects on her husband's political career, noting that he worked across party lines to help everyone in Hartford, regardless of political affiliation. She emphasizes that he was not selfish or partisan, and that his efforts made a positive impact on the city. The speaker continues to live by the principle of helping others and doing what she believes is right, without strict adherence to party lines.

lines.line: # Changing Demographics and Attitudes Among Migrants

> The speaker reflects on how the profile of migrants has shifted from the 1950s and 1960s to more recent times. Older men were once eager to work on farms in the United States, motivated by the opportunity to earn money for their families or invest in their own farms back home. In contrast, younger generations are less interested in agricultural labor, preferring the allure of American fashion and lifestyle over hard, low-paying work in fields like sugarcane or tobacco. This generational change has led to fewer young people seeking these traditional migrant labor opportunities.

metadata.title: Changing Demographics and Attitudes Among Migrants
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on how the profile of migrants has shifted from the 1950s and 1960s to more recent times. Older men were once eager to work on farms in the United States, motivated by the opportunity to earn money for their families or invest in their own farms back home. In contrast, younger generations are less interested in agricultural labor, preferring the allure of American fashion and lifestyle over hard, low-paying work in fields like sugarcane or tobacco. This generational change has led to fewer young people seeking these traditional migrant labor opportunities.

lines.line: # Youth Aspirations and Economic Challenges in the Caribbean

> Instead of pursuing farm work or migration, many young people in the Caribbean are seeking quick ways to make money, such as through music or other fast-money ventures. The speaker expresses concern about the lack of job opportunities, especially as migration routes to places like London, Canada, and the United States have become more restricted. She describes the troubling sight of large numbers of unemployed youth and the absence of factories or other employment options, leading to uncertainty about their future.

metadata.title: Youth Aspirations and Economic Challenges in the Caribbean

The speaker expresses concern about the lack of job opportunities, especially as migration routes to places like London, Canada, and the United States have become more restricted. She describes the troubling sight of large numbers of unemployed youth and the absence of factories or other employment options, leading to uncertainty about their future.

lines.line: # Community Involvement and Personal Commitments

> The speaker discusses her limited involvement in organizations due to her responsibilities with her family, church, and business. She is active in her church and supports the CNA program started by her husband. Much of her time is devoted to managing a family-owned building, which has been successfully rented out since 1973. While she is a member of the Council of Negro Women, her participation is limited by her other commitments.

metadata.title: Community Involvement and Personal Commitments

While she is a member of the Council of Negro Women, her participation is limited by her other commitments.

lines.line: # West Indian and Caribbean Organizations: Roles and Distinctions

> The speaker supports the activities of the West Indian Social Club but is not deeply involved due to time constraints and her personal disposition. She notes her husband's leadership role as president of the Caribbean Club and distinguishes between the agendas and programs of the West Indian Social Club and the Caribbean American society. Each organization has its own focus, such as scholarships, beauty pageants, and other community events, reflecting the diversity of interests within the Caribbean community.

Each organization has its own focus, such as scholarships, beauty pageants, and other community events, reflecting the diversity of interests within the Caribbean community.

lines.line: # Unity and Division Within the West Indian Community

> The conversation explores the challenges and possibilities of uniting the various West Indian organizations under a single voice. The speaker believes that unity is possible and desirable, emphasizing the importance of avoiding division and working together for common goals. She acknowledges that differing opinions exist but stresses the need for collective action and shared purpose to strengthen the community.

metadata.title: Unity and Division Within the West Indian Community
metadata.synopsis: The conversation explores the challenges and possibilities of uniting the various West Indian organizations under a single voice. The speaker believes that unity is possible and desirable, emphasizing the importance of avoiding division and working together for common goals. She acknowledges that differing opinions exist but stresses the need for collective action and shared purpose to strengthen the community.

lines.line: # Identity and Multiculturalism in Hartford

> The speaker does not single herself out as Jamaican or West Indian but sees herself as part of a diverse society, serving people of all backgrounds. She highlights the multicultural environment of Hartford and the importance of inclusivity. Despite the dispersion of West Indians into the suburbs and their integration into broader society, she believes that cultural events and shared celebrations can still bring the community together and foster unity.

metadata.title: Identity and Multiculturalism in Hartford

She highlights the multicultural environment of Hartford and the importance of inclusivity. Despite the dispersion of West Indians into the suburbs and their integration into broader society, she believes that cultural events and shared celebrations can still bring the community together and foster unity.

lines.line: # Political Engagement and Community Advancement

> The speaker expresses a desire for greater political presence and influence for West Indians in the area. She believes that political engagement is essential for advocating for the community's needs and ensuring their contributions are recognized. Cultivating good leadership and active participation in civic life are seen as key to improving the community's standing and making a positive impact.

metadata.title: Political Engagement and Community Advancement

She believes that political engagement is essential for advocating for the community's needs and ensuring their contributions are recognized. lines.line: # Party Politics and Voting Patterns Among Black Communities

> The speaker discusses the historical tendency for African Americans and West Indians to support the Democratic Party, noting that running as a Republican was once seen as unusual or even controversial. She emphasizes the importance of a two-party system for healthy democracy and recalls her husband's efforts to work across party lines for the good of the community. Ultimately, she values doing what is right over strict party allegiance and believes in helping others regardless of political affiliation.

Cultivating good leadership and active participation in civic life are seen as key to improving the community's standing and making a positive impact.
# Party Politics and Voting Patterns Among Black Communities

> The speaker discusses the historical tendency for African Americans and West Indians to support the Democratic Party, noting that running as a Republican was once seen as unusual or even controversial. She emphasizes the importance of a two-party system for healthy democracy and recalls her husband's efforts to work across party lines for the good of the community. Ultimately, she values doing what is right over strict party allegiance and believes in helping others regardless of political affiliation.

story.title: Molly Bennett Part 2
story.description: The interview with Molly Bennett explores the shifts in migrant demographics from the 1950s and 60s to the present, highlighting generational differences in work preferences and economic opportunities. Bennett discusses the challenges facing the West Indian community in Hartford, including the scarcity of jobs and the importance of political involvement for community empowerment. She also stresses the significance of unity among Caribbean organizations and the potential for collective action. Bennett's perspective emphasizes inclusivity beyond ethnic identity, focusing on community service and the historical contributions of her late husband, Colin Bennett, to local politics and inter-party collaboration.
